questions.prompt: Why is `useMemo(() => items.filter(i => i.active), [items])` on a 10-item list usually pointless?

choices.text: The 'computation' is cheap — filtering 10 items takes microseconds. useMemo itself has overhead (storing the value, comparing deps on every render). The overhead exceeds the savings; net negative on performance. useMemo helps when: the computation is genuinely expensive (sorting 10k items, complex math, parsing), or the result must be referentially stable as a dep for another effect/memo. For trivial computations, drop the useMemo — code is simpler and faster. React Compiler in React 19 makes this even more true; manual memoization becomes mostly unnecessary.

questions.explanation: useMemo overhead > cheap computation cost. Only memoize genuinely expensive work.

questions.prompt: Parent passes `config = { theme }` (inline) to a `React.memo`'d child. Child re-renders every parent render despite memo. Cause?

choices.text: `config` is a new object reference every parent render. React.memo does shallow equality on props; new reference means 'different,' so the child re-renders despite memo. Fix: wrap the config in useMemo at the parent — `const config = useMemo(() => ({ theme }), [theme])`. Now config is stable when theme is stable; the child's memo actually skips re-renders. Same trap with inline function props (`onClick={() => ...}`) — wrap in useCallback if the child is memoized. The lesson: memoizing the child without stabilizing its props is wasted.

questions.explanation: Inline objects are new refs every render. Wrap in useMemo at the parent to stabilize.
